question: Unit FDV above $3B one day after launch?
description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if the Fully Diluted Valuation of Unit's governance token is greater than the value specified in the title 1 day after launch. Otherwise, the market will resolve to "No."

The token must be actively, publicly transferable and tradable to be considered a launch.

The FDV will be determined using the total token supply multiplied by the token price.

"1 day after launch" is defined as 4:00 PM ET on the calendar day following launch. The resolution source for this market is the most liquid price source available. If Unit (https://hyperunit.xyz/) doesn't launch a token by December 31, 2027, 11:59 PM ET, this market will resolve to "No".

question: Canada's population Up or Down this year?
description:
This market will resolve to "Up" if the population of Canada in the 4th quarter of 2026 is greater than the population of Canada in the 4th quarter of 2025.

This market will resolve to "Down" if the population of Canada in the 4th quarter of 2026 is less than the population of Canada in the 4th quarter of 2025.

If the population in the 4th quarter of 2025 and the population in the 4th quarter of 2026 are exactly equal, this market will resolve to 50-50.

If the relevant quarterly population estimate release is not available by April 30, 2027, 11:59PM ET, this market will resolve to 50-50.

The resolution source for this market will be the quarterly population estimates release for the 4th quarter of 2026 from StatCan (https://www.statcan.gc.ca/en/start). 

Note: data from the initial release of the referenced population estimate report is what will be used to resolve this market. Data may be revised during the following quarter or as a part of the next estimate's publication, however any revisions to population estimate report data made after the initial release of the specified report will not be considered for this market's resolution.

If the population in the 4th quarter of 2025 and the population in the 4th quarter of 2026 are exactly equal, this market will resolve to 50-50.

If the relevant quarterly population estimate release is not available by April 30, 2027, 11:59PM ET, this market will resolve to 50-50.

Note: data from the initial release of the referenced population estimate report is what will be used to resolve this market. Data may be revised during the following quarter or as a part of the next estimate's publication, however any revisions to population estimate report data made after the initial release of the specified report will not be considered for this market's resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent Statistics Canada data for Q3 2024 shows Canada's population growth slowed to 0.2%—the weakest quarterly pace since late 2020—with non-permanent residents declining for the first time since 2019 amid falling study and work permits. The federal government's October 24 Immigration Levels Plan slashed permanent resident targets to 395,000 for 2025 from prior highs, aiming to reduce temporary residents by over 400,000 in three years amid housing shortages and public backlash. These policy shifts, coupled with reduced visa approvals and potential provincial restrictions, have traders pricing a 61% implied probability of overall population decline by year-end, reflecting consensus on decelerating net migration despite earlier robust gains.

question: Will the Republican Party win the AK-AL House seat?
description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the AK-AL congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

events.description:

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader sentiment favors Republicans at 68% implied probability for Alaska's at-large House seat, driven by recent polls showing GOP nominee Nick Begich leading incumbent Democrat Mary Peltola by 4-7 points in surveys from Emerson and others conducted in late October 2024. Begich's edge stems from a unified Republican field after the August primary, former President Trump's endorsement, and strong fundraising, contrasting Peltola's narrow 2022 ranked-choice victory amid divided conservative votes. Recent developments include Begich dropping his legal challenge to ranked-choice voting, clearing uncertainty, and early voting underway with higher GOP turnout signals. Democrats hold at 29.5% on Peltola's incumbency and Alaska Native support, though polls indicate her path narrowing absent a late surge. Upcoming general election on November 5 could shift dynamics via final ballot exhaustion under ranked-choice rules.

question: Ilhan Omar federally charged by March 31?
description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if the United States federal government formally charges or announces a criminal indictment of Ilhan Omar by March 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

For the purposes of this market, the District of Columbia and any county, municipality, or other subdivision of a State shall be included within the definition of a State.

The primary resolution source for this market will be official information from US governmental sources; however, a wide consensus of credible reporting will also be used.

events.title: Ilhan Omar federally charged by March 31?
events.description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if the United States federal government formally charges or announces a criminal indictment of Ilhan Omar by March 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

For the purposes of this market, the District of Columbia and any county, municipality, or other subdivision of a State shall be included within the definition of a State.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus heavily favors no federal charges against Rep. Ilhan Omar by March 31, driven by the complete absence of any announced Department of Justice investigation or indictment proceedings. Past allegations of immigration irregularities and campaign finance issues, resurfacing amid her narrow August 2024 primary win, have prompted Republican calls for probes but yielded no action from federal authorities. With the Trump administration's January inauguration still months away and Senate confirmations for key DOJ roles pending, the tight timeline reinforces skepticism. Realistic shifts could arise from accelerated incoming leadership prioritizing an expedited case, though no primary sources indicate momentum.

question: Will Lighter reach $3 before 2027?
description:
This market will immediately resolve to “Yes” if any Lighter 1-minute candle for Lighter (LIT/USDC) between January 27, 2026, 14:25 and December 31, 2026, 23:59 ET has a final “High” price equal to or greater than the price specified in the title. Otherwise, the market will resolve to “No.”

The resolution source for this market is Lighter, specifically the LIT/USDC “High” prices available at:
https://app.lighter.xyz/trade/LIT_USDC
with the chart set to “1m” (one-minute candles) on the top bar.

Only the Lighter LIT/USDC price feed will be used.
Prices from other exchanges, trading pairs, or spot markets will not be considered.

events.title: What price will Lighter hit in 2026?
events.description: What price will Lighter hit before 2027?

question: Israeli parliament dissolved by June 30?
description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if the sitting Israeli Knesset (Israel's parliament), as of this market's inception, is dissolved between January 27 and June 30, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

The primary resolution source for this market is official information from the government of Israel; however, a consensus of credible reporting will also be used.

events.title: Israeli parliament dissolved by...?
events.description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if the sitting Israeli Knesset (Israel's parliament), as of this market's inception, is dissolved between September 3 and October 31, 2025, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

The primary resolution source for this market is official information from the government of Israel; however, a consensus of credible reporting will also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader sentiment on Israeli Knesset dissolution centers on Prime Minister Netanyahu's coalition maintaining a narrow 64-seat majority amid escalating tensions over ultra-Orthodox military draft exemptions and the March 2025 state budget deadline, where failure risks government collapse. Recent developments include the opposition's June 2024 dissolution bill defeat, Benny Gantz's exit from the war cabinet, and Netanyahu surviving October 2024 no-confidence votes, all amid Gaza war pressures and protests eroding public support. Polls favoring opposition parties like National Unity reflect rising snap election risks, while upcoming draft law and budget votes could prompt defections, directly shaping market-implied probabilities.

question: Dutch House of Representatives dissolved in 2026?
description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if the sitting Dutch House of Representatives (Tweede Kamer der Staten-Generaal), as of January 27, 2026, is dissolved by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

The primary resolution source for this market is official information from the government of the Netherlands, however a consensus of credible reporting will also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: The stability of the Netherlands' Schoof cabinet, formed in July 2024 after prolonged coalition talks among PVV, VVD, NSC, and BBB, anchors trader consensus at 81.5% against House of Representatives dissolution in 2026. This four-party government has navigated early tests, including passage of a strict asylum emergency law in October 2024 amid public protests, and survived a heated November budget debate without defections. Absent major coalition fractures or crises—none evident in official statements or votes—a snap election remains unlikely before the full term ends around 2027. Traders weigh this against historical precedents of prolonged Dutch minority governments, pricing in low near-term disruption risk.

question: Will Google Gemini score at least 40% on the FrontierMath Benchmark?
description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if any Google Gemini model achieves the listed score or greater on the FrontierMath Exam by June 30, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, the market will resolve to "No".

This market will resolve according to the Epoch AI’s Frontier Math benchmarking leaderboard (https://epoch.ai/frontiermath) for Tier 1-3. Studies which are not included in the leaderboard (e.g. https://x.com/EpochAIResearch/status/1945905796904005720) will not be considered.

The primary resolution source will be information from EpochAI; however, a consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

events.title: Google Gemini score on FrontierMath Benchmark by June 30?
events.description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if any Google Gemini model achieves the listed score or greater on the FrontierMath Exam by June 30, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, the market will resolve to "No".

This market will resolve according to the Epoch AI’s Frontier Math benchmarking leaderboard (https://epoch.ai/frontiermath) for Tier 1-3. Studies which are not included in the leaderboard (e.g. https://x.com/EpochAIResearch/status/1945905796904005720) will not be considered.

The primary resolution source will be information from EpochAI; however, a consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket reflects skepticism about Google Gemini achieving a breakthrough on the FrontierMath benchmark by June 30, with implied probabilities hovering below 20% for surpassing the current state-of-the-art around 4-5% held by OpenAI's o1 models. Gemini 2.0 variants, including Flash and Experimental releases from December 2024, have posted sub-3% scores on this Epoch AI test of 179 ultra-hard math problems, underscoring persistent reasoning gaps despite massive compute scaling. Competitive pressure intensifies from Anthropic's Claude 3.5 and xAI's Grok-3 previews, while Google's I/O 2025 (typically early May) looms as the key catalyst for any Gemini 2.5 announcement—watch for verified leaderboard updates, as private evals often precede public claims. Timelines slip, but trader focus remains on demonstrated, reproducible gains.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader sentiment on OpenAI achieving a breakthrough FrontierMath score by June 30 leans bearish, driven by o1-preview's meager 1.6% accuracy on this 177-problem benchmark of competition-level math, released by METR in November 2024 to test frontier AI limits. While OpenAI's "reasoning" models like o1 show gains over GPT-4o's near-zero performance, experts project solutions only by 2026-2027 amid unsolved IMO-hard problems. Competitive pressure from Anthropic's Claude 3.5 Sonnet (0.5%) and Google's Gemini intensifies, but no GPT-5 or o2 launch is confirmed before summer. Watch OpenAI's December updates and Q1 2025 earnings for training compute hints; slipping timelines historically cap implied probabilities below 20%.

eventMetadata.context_description: Alabama's 1st Congressional District, a solidly Republican area with a Cook Partisan Voting Index of R+16, drives the heavy trader consensus favoring the Republican candidate at 92.5%, reflecting Barry Moore's incumbency and strong primary performance in March 2024 against fellow conservatives. Recent forecasts from nonpartisan raters like Cook Political Report label the seat as "Solid Republican," with scant polling and historical Trump landslides (67% in 2020) underscoring minimal Democratic viability against challenger Thomas Davis. Realistic challenges include a major scandal engulfing Moore, unforeseen legal hurdles, or an extraordinary Democratic turnout surge tied to national dynamics, though district fundamentals limit such shifts. Traders price in low upset risk ahead of November balloting.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Alabama's 1st Congressional District, a solidly Republican area with a Cook Partisan Voting Index of R+16, drives the heavy trader consensus favoring the Republican candidate at 92.5%, reflecting Barry Moore's incumbency and strong primary performance in March 2024 against fellow conservatives. Recent forecasts from nonpartisan raters like Cook Political Report label the seat as "Solid Republican," with scant polling and historical Trump landslides (67% in 2020) underscoring minimal Democratic viability against challenger Thomas Davis. Realistic challenges include a major scandal engulfing Moore, unforeseen legal hurdles, or an extraordinary Democratic turnout surge tied to national dynamics, though district fundamentals limit such shifts. Traders price in low upset risk ahead of November balloting.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus strongly favors the Democratic Party at 90.5% odds to win Alabama's 2nd Congressional District House seat, anchored by incumbent Terri Sewell's dominant position in the court-redrawn Black-majority district (51.7% Black voting-age population). Recent Emerson College polling (October 2024) shows Sewell leading Republican Caroleene Dobson 57%-35%, consistent with the district's D+15 partisan lean, her history of 20+ point victories, and ratings from Cook Political Report as "Solid Democratic." No major scandals or shifts have emerged, bolstering trader confidence. Realistic challenges include low Democratic turnout in a midterm or unexpected GOP mobilization, but base rates in comparable districts indicate slim upset odds.

Realistic challenges include a major Rogers scandal, health setback, or unprecedented Democratic turnout wave, though evidence shows low probability amid stable national headwinds.

eventMetadata.context_description: Republican incumbent Rep. Robert Aderholt's longstanding dominance drives the 94.5% trader consensus for a GOP hold in Alabama's 4th congressional district, a reliably red seat with a Cook Partisan Voter Index of R+33 where Trump carried 72% in 2020. Aderholt, first elected in 1996, faced no primary opposition and crushed his 2022 foe 98%-2%, bolstered by superior fundraising and local name recognition against underfunded Democrat Anthony Hughes. Recent quiet polling and forecaster ratings (Safe Republican by Cook, Race to the WH) reinforce this lopsided outlook amid early voting. Realistic challenges include a major Aderholt scandal or improbable Democratic turnout surge, though historical base rates suggest scant risk before November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Barry Moore's entrenched position in Alabama's solidly conservative 5th Congressional District drives trader consensus at 91.5% for a GOP hold. This northwest Alabama seat, with a partisan lean of R+15 and Trump carrying it by 37 points in 2020, favors Moore amid recent polls showing him leading Democrat Paul King by wide margins—often 55% to 25%—supported by strong fundraising and grassroots momentum. No scandals or legal hurdles have disrupted his campaign, aligning with historical safe Republican holds in similar districts. Realistic upset scenarios hinge on a sudden Moore controversy or improbable national Democratic wave, but evidence points to stability through Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Barry Moore dominates trader sentiment for Alabama's 6th Congressional District House race, with markets implying 93.5% odds for a GOP win driven by the district's deep-red partisan lean (R+16 Cook PVI) and Moore's decisive March primary victory over challenger John Roberts. Wooten by 30+ points, bolstered by superior fundraising and minimal Democratic infrastructure in this suburban Birmingham area. No major catalysts have emerged to erode GOP advantages post-primaries. Realistic challenges include a sudden Republican scandal, national Democratic surge, or legal hurdles before the November general election, though base rates for upsets in such districts remain under 10%.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Republican incumbent Rick Crawford holds a commanding lead in Arkansas's 1st Congressional District House race, driving 94% trader consensus on a GOP victory, anchored by the district's deep-red partisan lean (Cook PVI R+16) and Crawford's decade-plus tenure with consistent 60%+ wins. Recent polling from Race to the WH and others shows him ahead 58-35% against Democrat Laura Anthony, bolstered by President Trump's 2024 district sweep and minimal fundraising gap. Early voting trends favor Republicans, with no major scandals or shifts. Realistic challenges include a late Democratic surge via national headwinds, Crawford health issues, or turnout collapse, though base rates for safe seats flipping remain under 5%.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Rep. French Hill's commanding lead in Arkansas's 2nd Congressional District, rated R+15 by the Cook Partisan Voter Index, drives trader consensus to 88% for the GOP in the AR-02 House race. Hill secured over 60% in the March primary and boasts a 2022 general election margin above 30 points against Democrat Jay Martin, a retired Marine facing fundraising disadvantages. Recent FEC reports confirm Hill's $1.2 million cash-on-hand edge, while sparse district polling reinforces his double-digit leads. Absent scandals or national wave shifts, the race aligns with Arkansas's Republican stronghold patterns, with early voting underway but unlikely to alter implied probabilities.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus strongly favors the Republican Party at 90.5% in the Arkansas 3rd Congressional District House race, anchored by the district's deep Republican tilt (Cook PVI R+24) and incumbent Steve Womack's victory in a contested March primary, where he secured 42% against intra-party challengers like Gunner DeLay. Womack, holding the seat since 2011, has won general elections by wide margins—over 60% in recent cycles—bolstered by strong fundraising and alignment with GOP base priorities on border security and fiscal conservatism. No public polling indicates a competitive matchup against Democratic nominee Caitlin Draper. Scenarios to challenge this include a major Womack scandal, health issue, or unprecedented Democratic wave, but such shifts lack current evidence amid quiet campaign dynamics.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Bruce Westerman holds a commanding lead in Arkansas's 4th Congressional District House race, reflecting the district's deep Republican tilt (Cook Partisan Voter Index R+16) and his history of landslide victories, including 70% in 2022. Recent FEC filings show Westerman dominating fundraising with over $1 million cash-on-hand versus Democrat Noah Walker's modest $50,000, while nonpartisan forecasters like Cook Political Report rate AR-04 as solidly Republican with no competitive polling shifts. Trader consensus at 92.5% for Republicans underscores these structural advantages and weak Democratic turnout base. Realistic challenges include a major Westerman scandal, unexpected national Democratic wave, or late-breaking endorsement surge for Walker, though historical precedents suggest low probability before November.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Greg Stanton dominates trader sentiment in Arizona's 3rd Congressional District House race, with market odds implying a 92.5% win probability reflecting his consistent double-digit polling leads and the district's Democratic-leaning voter base in Phoenix suburbs. Recent factors include Stanton's fundraising edge—over $2 million raised versus Republican David Gillis's under $100,000—and no major campaign disruptions, aligning with 538's 93% forecast favoring Stanton. The Cook Partisan Voter Index rates AZ-03 as D+5, bolstering this consensus. Realistic challenges include a late GOP turnout surge, unforeseen Democratic scandal, or ballot irregularities, though historical base rates for safe seats suggest low upset risk ahead of Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the Arizona 4th Congressional District House race strongly favors Democrats at 87%, driven by incumbent Greg Stanton's commanding polling leads and the district's Democratic-leaning voter registration advantage in urban Phoenix suburbs. Recent public surveys, including those from internal campaigns and nonpartisan trackers, show Stanton ahead by 15-20 points over Republican nominee Dave Schmidt, a former state senator, with no momentum-shifting events like debates or scandals. Stanton's fundraising superiority—over $1.5 million raised versus Schmidt's under $400,000—further solidifies the edge. District demographics and incumbency reinforce this positioning, though early voting starting mid-October could affect turnout dynamics.

eventMetadata.context_description: Arizona's 8th Congressional District, a reliably Republican seat with a Cook Partisan Voter Index of R+13, drives trader consensus heavily favoring the GOP nominee at 85%. Abe Hamadeh secured the Republican nomination in the August 6 primary, topping a competitive field including Ben Quayle and Peter Tate with 33% of the vote, solidifying his position as a Trump-aligned conservative. Democrat Joe Heston advances unopposed from his primary but trails in fundraising and name recognition amid the district's history of double-digit GOP margins—Trump carried it by 21 points in 2020. Nonpartisan ratings from Cook Political Report and Inside Elections deem it Solid Republican, with no recent polls indicating a contest; early voting begins October 9, but fundamentals point to minimal upset risk.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Paul Gosar dominates trader sentiment at 87% implied probability for Arizona's 9th Congressional District House seat, driven by the district's strong Republican lean (Cook PVI R+7) and his history of double-digit victories. Gosar cruised through the August primary with 88% of the vote, facing Democrat Quynh Tran, who won her primary narrowly amid low turnout. Recent nonpartisan ratings from Cook Political Report and Inside Elections label the race "Safe Republican," with no public polls showing competitiveness; Gosar's fundraising edge—over $1 million cash-on-hand—further solidifies his position. Democratic odds at 10.5% reflect base uncertainty ahead of the November 5 general election, but no catalysts have emerged to shift the consensus.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus assigns a 94% implied probability to the Democratic Party prevailing in California's 1st Congressional District House race, despite nonpartisan forecasters rating the seat Safe Republican amid its R+10 partisan voting index. Incumbent Doug LaMalfa (R), seeking a seventh term, dominated the March top-two primary with 52% and has secured general election victories by 25-30 point margins in a region Donald Trump carried decisively in 2020. Challenger David Peterson (D) advanced with 24% but trails significantly in fundraising, with no public polls indicating competitiveness. Early voting began October 28, but absent scandals, turnout surges, or national Democratic momentum, Republican retention remains the base case.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Jared Huffman's commanding poll lead over Republican Chris Coulombe fuels the 91.5% trader consensus for a Democratic victory in California's 2nd Congressional District House race. A recent Emerson College poll (Oct. 23-24) shows Huffman at 52% to Coulombe's 28%, aligning with the district's D+9 partisan voter index and unanimous "Safe Democratic" ratings from forecasters like Cook Political Report and Sabato's Crystal Ball. Huffman's long tenure since 2013, superior fundraising, and the district's blue-leaning North Coast demographics solidify this edge, with early voting trends favoring Democrats. Realistic challenges include a late GOP turnout surge or unforeseen Huffman scandal, though current evidence suggests low likelihood before Election Day.

Incumbent Tom McClintock's retirement opened the race, but no major GOP catalysts have emerged. Upsets could stem from a late Republican ad surge, voter turnout drop-off, or unforeseen scandal, though current evidence points to low upset risk ahead of Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus heavily favors a Democratic victory in California's 6th Congressional District House race at 89%, driven by Democrat Adam Gray's consistent polling lead over incumbent Republican John Duarte. Duarte's narrow 2022 win (50.5%) contrasts with current headwinds from voter dissatisfaction on agriculture policy and inflation. With ballots still being counted post-Election Day, uncalled tallies keep minor GOP odds alive, but Gray's momentum reflects traders' assessment of district fundamentals favoring Democrats.

question: Will the Republican Party win the CA-10 House seat?
description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the CA-10 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the California 12th congressional district House race strongly favors the Democratic nominee at 92% implied probability, propelled by consistent double-digit polling leads for Adam Gray over incumbent Republican John Duarte. Recent aggregates from outlets like Race to the WH and 538 show Gray ahead 51-43 on average, fueled by superior fundraising—Gray raised over $3 million versus Duarte's $1.5 million—and robust Democratic turnout efforts in the Latino-heavy Central Valley. Duarte's slim 2022 win has faded amid voter concerns over agriculture policy and inflation. Realistic upset scenarios include a GOP ballot harvesting surge, independent voter swing, or a Gray scandal, but no major catalysts have emerged ahead of Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Kevin Mullin holds a commanding lead in California's 14th Congressional District House race, reflected in trader consensus pricing at 93% for Democrats, driven by the district's strong Democratic lean in the Bay Area Peninsula—where President Biden won by over 30 points in 2020—and Mullin's 2022 victory margin exceeding 35 points. Upcoming general election dynamics could shift sentiment if late-breaking events emerge.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Eric Swalwell's commanding 73% primary win on June 4 solidified trader consensus for a Democratic hold in California's 15th congressional district, a reliably blue Bay Area seat with strong Democratic registration advantages. eventMetadata.context_description: In California's 16th Congressional District, a heavily Democratic Bay Area seat where Joe Biden won 70% in 2020, the top-two primary system propelled former San Jose Mayor Sam Liccardo and State Sen. Joe Simitian—both Democrats—into the November general election after retiring Rep. Anna Eshoo's departure, cementing trader consensus at 94% for a Democratic Party win. Recent polling reinforces this, showing comfortable Democratic margins amid minimal Republican viability following primary also-ran Sarah Davis's 12% share. Markets price the slim 5% Republican shot reflecting extreme longshots like dual Democratic disqualifications or ballot irregularities, though no such catalysts have emerged ahead of early voting and debates.

This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the CA-18 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus heavily favors the Democratic candidate at 94% implied probability in the CA-18 House race, driven by long-serving incumbent Zoe Lofgren's stronghold in this solidly Democratic Silicon Valley district, where Joe Biden won by 37 points in 2020 and Lofgren has secured 70%+ victories historically. Recent primary results saw Lofgren advance unchallenged against a low-profile Republican, Scott Cummings, amid vast fundraising disparities and no competitive polling. This reflects the district's D+25 partisan lean per Cook PVI, insulating it from national GOP momentum. Realistic challenges would require an unforeseen Lofgren scandal, her withdrawal, or an extraordinary Republican surge in California turnout—scenarios with minimal precedent in this safe seat ahead of Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Jim Costa's commanding lead in California's 19th Congressional District House race drives the 93.5% trader consensus for a Democratic win, fueled by recent polling averages showing him ahead by 20+ points against Republican challenger Michael Fitzgerald. Costa's primary dominance (over 54% in the top-two system) underscores his entrenched voter base in this Central Valley district with its mix of agricultural interests and growing Hispanic electorate, bolstered by superior fundraising and no major scandals. While national Republican momentum could theoretically narrow gaps, realistic challenges would require a Fitzgerald surge via unprecedented turnout or a Costa misstep, though historical base rates for such incumbents favor stability ahead of November balloting.

eventMetadata.context_description: Republican incumbent Vince Fong's commanding lead in California's 20th Congressional District House race drives the 91.5% trader consensus for a GOP victory, bolstered by his decisive May special election win with nearly 68% of the vote and consistent double-digit polling advantages over Democrat Marisa Wood in recent surveys from outlets like Inside Elections and Race to the WH. The district's R+5 partisan lean and Fong's endorsement from former Speaker Kevin McCarthy further solidify his position amid low Democratic turnout prospects in this Central Valley battleground. Realistic challenges include a late Democratic fundraising surge, national blue wave momentum, or unforeseen candidate scandal shifting undecided voters, though current evidence shows minimal movement.

eventMetadata.context_description: Democrat Adam Gray's consistent polling lead over incumbent Republican David Valadao has solidified trader consensus at 89% for a Democratic win in California's 21st Congressional District, reflecting the Central Valley race's tight but tilting dynamics. Recent Emerson College polling shows Gray ahead 49%-44% among likely voters, bolstered by his fundraising edge—over $7 million raised versus Valadao's $4 million—and stronger support among Latino voters, who comprise over half the district. Valadao's narrow 2022 victory by 1.5 points leaves him vulnerable amid national headwinds for Republicans, though early voting turnout remains even. Traders price in Gray's momentum from October debates and endorsements, with odds implying low upset risk absent major shifts before Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Vince Fong's commanding position in California's 23rd Congressional District drives the 86.5% trader consensus for a GOP victory, bolstered by his decisive special election win earlier this year and strong primary showing with 45% of the vote against Democrat Marisa Wood's 28%. Recent polls, including a September Berkeley IGS survey showing Fong ahead 54%-34%, underscore his double-digit leads in this R+4 leaning district, amplified by superior fundraising—Fong raised over $1.5 million versus Wood's under $300,000. No major Democratic surges or scandals have emerged ahead of the November 5 ballot, reinforcing Republican dominance amid steady early voting trends.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Salud Carbajal's double-digit leads in recent polls drive the 92% trader consensus for a Democratic victory in California's 24th Congressional District, a coastal seat with a D+4 partisan lean encompassing Santa Barbara and San Luis Obispo counties. Carbajal's fundraising superiority and 2022 reelection by 10 points reinforce this edge against Republican challenger Ron Hopper, who lags despite national GOP momentum on issues like inflation. District demographics favoring Democrats, stable voter registration, and minimal late-campaign shifts sustain the lopsided odds. Realistic challenges include a sudden scandal hitting Carbajal or unexpected high Republican turnout, though evidence points to continuity ahead of Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: In California's 28th Congressional District, trader consensus heavily favors the Democratic Party at 91% implied probability for the House election winner, driven by the top-two primary system advancing two Democrats—State Assemblymember Steve Rodriguez and Chris Mathis—to the November 5 general election ballot, while Republican David Luo placed third and was eliminated. This structural lock in a district with a strong Democratic lean (Cook PVI D+14) and incumbent Judy Chu's retirement amplifies the frontrunner edge, reinforced by Rodriguez's polling lead (e.g., 42% to Mathis's 28% in recent internal surveys) and fundraising dominance. Realistic challenges remain slim but could arise from a disqualifying scandal on the Democratic nominees or an unlikely surge in Republican write-ins, though historical precedents in similar races show near-zero upset rates.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Tony Cárdenas commands a commanding lead in California's 29th Congressional District, a solidly blue seat with a D+27 partisan lean, driving 93% trader consensus for a Democratic hold. Cárdenas, serving since 2013, dominated the March primary with 72% of the vote against minimal opposition, bolstered by superior fundraising—over $1 million raised versus under $100,000 for Republican nominee Steve Williams—and no competitive general election polls. District demographics in the Los Angeles-area favor Democrats heavily among Latino and working-class voters. Realistic challenges include a major Cárdenas scandal, unprecedented GOP turnout surge amid national tailwinds, or late-breaking endorsement shifts, though historical base rates for such safe seats suggest low upset risk ahead of November 5.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Democrat Joe Tran's commanding lead in recent CA-39 polls drives the 90.5% trader consensus for a Democratic House winner, reflecting his surge past Republican incumbent Young Kim following a razor-thin June primary. Nonpartisan aggregates like the Cook Political Report now rate the Orange County district as leaning Democratic, bolstered by Tran's superior fundraising—over $3 million versus Kim's $2.2 million—and endorsements from EMILY's List and local Vietnamese-American leaders in this diverse constituency. National headwinds for Republicans further tilt sentiment. Realistic challengers include a strong debate performance by Kim, fresh GOP attack ads on Tran's prosecutorial record, or a broader Republican ballot surge, though traders price these at just 8.5% odds amid steady polling gains.

question: Texas A&M Aggies vs. Alabama Crimson Tide
description:
In the upcoming CBB game, scheduled for February 3 at 12:00 AM ET:

If the Texas A&M Aggies win, the market will resolve to "Texas A&M Aggies".

If the Alabama Crimson Tide win, the market will resolve to "Alabama Crimson Tide".

If the game is postponed, this market will remain open until the game has been completed.

If the game is canceled entirely, with no make-up game, this market will resolve 50-50.

The result will be determined based on the final score including any overtime periods.

events.title: Texas A&M Aggies vs. Alabama Crimson Tide
events.description:
This event is for the CBB game between Texas A&M Aggies and Alabama Crimson Tide on February 3 at 12:00 AM ET.

If the game is postponed, this market will remain open until the game has been completed.

If the game is canceled entirely, with no make-up game, this market will resolve 50-50.

eventMetadata.context_description: Alabama's 75.6% implied probability reflects trader consensus on the Crimson Tide's defensive edge and home-field dominance in Tuscaloosa, where they've won 92% of SEC games since 2020. Jalen Milroe's recent form—1,200 passing yards, 300 rushing since Week 6—exploits Texas A&M's secondary, ranked 80th nationally against the pass, amid injuries to CB Will Lee III (out, knee). A&M's ground attack with Le'Veon Moss (800 yards) poses a threat, but Alabama's front seven, led by LT Overton (8 sacks), has held opponents under 100 rushing yards in four of six. Tide's 5-1 SEC record contrasts A&M's 4-2 skid against top-25 foes, with historical 8-2 edge since 2012 justifying the line.

Odds could shift on Friday's official injury report—Aggies' Isaac Johnson (questionable, ankle) out hurts depth, while Lobos' JT Topin (probable) bolsters frontcourt; momentum from Utah State's three-game win streak holds firm unless travel fatigue hits.

Trader consensus reflects these trends amid WCC playoff positioning.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus heavily favors the Democratic Party at 92.5% implied probability in the CA-41 House race, driven by frontrunner Will Rollins's consistent polling leads over incumbent Republican Ken Calvert, including a recent UC Berkeley survey showing Rollins ahead 49%-44% and internal Democratic polls indicating wider margins up to 10 points. The district, centered in Riverside County with a slight Republican PVI lean, has trended Democratic since Biden's 2020 win by 5 points, bolstered by Rollins's fundraising advantage—over $3 million raised versus Calvert's $1.5 million—and strong early vote turnout favoring Democrats. Scenarios challenging this include a late Republican mobilization surge, unexpected voter backlash to Rollins's record as Riverside County supervisor, or national GOP tailwinds shifting turnout dynamics before November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in California's 42nd congressional district House race strongly backs the Democratic candidate at 93%, driven by Will Rollins' consistent double-digit leads in recent polls over incumbent Republican Ken Calvert, including a 52%-42% edge in the latest Emerson survey. Rollins benefits from superior fundraising—over $3.5 million raised versus Calvert's $1.8 million—and the district's D+4 partisan lean, fueled by suburban voter shifts in Riverside County. Calvert's 30-year tenure draws criticism on ethics and abortion stances amid a competitive national environment. Realistic challenges include a late GOP turnout surge, Democratic ballot mishaps, or unforeseen scandals, though no such developments have materialized ahead of Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus heavily favors the Democratic Party at 93% for California's 43rd Congressional District House seat, driven by incumbent Maxine Waters' commanding 73% primary win in March and the district's deep Democratic tilt (Cook PVI D+27) in South Los Angeles, where registered Democrats outnumber Republicans over 5-to-1. No recent polls or catalysts indicate momentum for Republican nominee Steve Williams, who garnered 27% in the primary amid low GOP turnout. Realistic challenges would require a major scandal hitting Waters, unprecedented voter shifts, or external national wave effects, though historical precedents in safe seats like CA-43 make such outcomes unlikely before the November 5 ballot.

Realistic challenges include a late Republican enthusiasm surge, as seen in 2022, damaging Min revelations, or unexpected ballot curing issues tightening the race before November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Yadira Caraveo's double-digit leads in recent polls, including a 12-point edge in the October RMG Research survey, anchor the 93.5% trader consensus for a Democratic hold in Colorado's 2nd Congressional District House race. National headwinds for Republicans further solidify the pricing. Realistic challenges include a late GOP surge from Trump coattails, an unforeseen Caraveo scandal, or depressed Democratic turnout shifting the narrowly competitive race.

eventMetadata.context_description: In Colorado's 4th Congressional District House race, trader consensus favors Republican Lauren Boebert at 55% implied probability over Democrat Trisha Calvarese at 47%, reflecting the district's R+9 partisan lean tempered by Boebert's polarizing profile and narrow primary win. Dynamics remain balanced amid national headwinds for both parties, but separation could emerge from the October 17 debate, early voting trends starting October 21, or Trump campaign visits solidifying GOP base support.

eventMetadata.context_description: **Trader consensus favors the Republican Party at 64.5% in Colorado's 5th Congressional District House race**, reflecting the district's strong Republican tilt (R+9 Cook PVI, Trump +13 in 2020) and GOP nominee Jeff Crank's consistent polling leads over Democrat River Gassen. Recent catalysts include an October RMG Research poll showing Crank up 48%-42%, narrowing from summer gaps but still firm; Gassen gained from voter registration drives amid national Democratic turnout concerns. The October 17 debate looms as a potential volatility driver, though historical precedents in similar districts reinforce GOP resilience.

eventMetadata.context_description: Rosa DeLauro's entrenched incumbency since 1991 and dominant fundraising—over $1 million cash-on-hand versus Republican Matthew Corey's modest totals—drive the Democratic Party's 92% trader consensus in Connecticut's 3rd District House race. Realistic upset scenarios include a DeLauro scandal or massive Republican national wave, though historical base rates in safe seats make these improbable without fresh catalysts like October surprises.

Realistic challenges include a national Republican wave, unforeseen Democratic scandals, or depressed base turnout, though no such catalysts have emerged ahead of the November 5 general election.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Florida's 1st Congressional District, with its strong Republican tilt reflected in a Cook Partisan Voter Index of R+22, drives the 92.5% trader consensus for a GOP victory in the special election stemming from Matt Gaetz's resignation. Former state CFO Jimmy Patronis leads GOP primary polls at around 40%, bolstered by endorsements from Gaetz and fundraising edges over rivals like Joel Rudman, while Democrat Gay Valimont trails far behind amid historically weak Democratic performance in the Panhandle. Recent polls and early voting trends reinforce this dominance. Realistic challenges include a fragmented GOP primary yielding an underfunded nominee or an unforeseen scandal, though the district's base rates favor Republicans by wide margins ahead of the January 21 primaries.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Neal Dunn holds a commanding lead in Florida's 2nd Congressional District, a reliably red seat with a Cook Partisan Voter Index of R+19, driving trader consensus to 84% for the GOP. The district backed Donald Trump by 37 points in 2020, and nonpartisan forecasters like Cook Political Report and Inside Elections rate it Solid Republican. Recent polling from early October shows Dunn ahead 58%-35% against Democrat Gay Valimont, with no major shifts from fundraising edges or endorsements favoring Dunn. Early voting is underway ahead of November 5, but historical base rates for similar safe seats reinforce low upset risk, tempering Democratic odds at 15.5%.

eventMetadata.context_description: Florida's 3rd Congressional District, a solidly Republican area spanning conservative strongholds in Clay, Baker, Bradford, and Union counties plus Jacksonville suburbs, anchors trader consensus heavily favoring the Republican nominee at 87% implied probability. Incumbent Rep. Kat Cammack secured a decisive 72%-28% victory in 2022 amid a district with R+7 partisan lean and strong Trump support exceeding 20 points in 2020. Her unopposed August primary win and Democratic opponent Adianis Morales' limited profile sustain this edge, with no major scandals or shifts in recent polling. Traders eye November 5 general election voting, where historical base rates for similar safe seats reinforce low upset risk.

eventMetadata.context_description: Rep. Aaron Bean, the Republican incumbent, holds a commanding position in Florida's 4th Congressional District, a reliably conservative area spanning northeastern Florida with a strong GOP registration edge and Cook Partisan Voter Index of R+8. Bean's 2023 reelection bid features favorable historical margins—he won 65% in 2022—bolstered by superior fundraising, with over $500,000 cash on hand versus Democrat Betty Felder's modest totals. Recent primary wins for both nominees in August proceeded without surprises, and absent polls or scandals, trader consensus at 84.5% Republican reflects the district's entrenched conservative lean and low-flip risk, though general election turnout remains a wildcard ahead of November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: In Florida's 5th Congressional District, trader consensus heavily favors the Republican incumbent Kat Cammack at 86% implied probability, driven by the district's strong GOP lean (R+8 Cook PVI) and her dominant primary victory on August 20 with over 80% of the vote. Nonpartisan ratings from Cook Political Report and others classify the seat as Solid Republican, reflecting consistent GOP wins since redistricting. Democrat Joshua Weil advanced from a competitive primary but trails in fundraising and early internal polls showing Cammack up by 20+ points. Recent national Republican momentum in House battlegrounds and no major scandals further solidify the outlook ahead of the November 5 general election, with traders pricing in minimal upset risk.

eventMetadata.context_description: Randy Fine's decisive Republican primary victory in Florida's 8th Congressional District, a reliably conservative area Trump carried by over 30 points in 2020, anchors the 89.5% trader consensus for a GOP win. Incumbent Mike Waltz's departure to serve as Trump's national security adviser opened the seat, but Fine's strong fundraising and internal polling leads—often exceeding 20 points over Democrat Tim White—reinforce expectations of a hold. Recent developments, including Fine's August primary triumph and lack of Democratic momentum amid national headwinds, have widened the gap, with traders pricing in minimal upset risk ahead of November's general election.

eventMetadata.context_description: In Florida's 9th congressional district, a battleground spanning Orange and Osceola counties, trader consensus reflects a dead-even contest between incumbent Democrat Darren Soto and Republican challenger Mike Stanley, with recent polls like RMG Research showing them tied at 46-45%. Soto's incumbency and fundraising edge are offset by Stanley's military background and GOP momentum from statewide Republican voter registration gains. Tight early voting turnout and comparable grassroots efforts keep the race neck-and-neck, mirroring national House toss-up dynamics. Separation could emerge from final absentee ballot data, district-specific turnout disparities, or coattails from Florida Senate races, as traders price in high uncertainty ahead of Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Maxwell Frost's dominant position in Florida's 10th Congressional District underpins the 90% trader consensus favoring the Democratic Party in the House election winner market. Frost, the district's first Gen Z congressman representing the Orlando area, benefits from a D+8 partisan lean per Cook Political Report ratings, strong fundraising exceeding $1 million, and consistent polling leads, including a recent survey showing him ahead 55%-30% over Republican challenger Ruth Uribe. Frost's unchallenged August 20 Democratic primary victory solidified his momentum, while Uribe's primary win faced low turnout and limited resources, contributing to the GOP's slim 10.5% implied probability amid a stable national House landscape. Upcoming early voting could influence final trader sentiment.

eventMetadata.context_description: Rep. Daniel Webster's incumbency in Florida's 11th Congressional District, a reliably Republican area with a partisan lean of R+8, anchors the 88% trader consensus for a GOP hold. The district, spanning parts of Lake, Marion, and Sumter counties including the Villages retirement community, delivered Trump a 16-point win in 2020 and Webster 65% last cycle. All major forecasters rate it Safe Republican, with no competitive polling amid limited Democratic investment. Webster cruised through an unopposed August primary, while nominee Barbie Hunley Hall faces steep fundraising and turnout hurdles. Absent national wave shifts or surprises before November 5, traders see minimal upset risk.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Vern Buchanan's strong hold on Florida's 12th Congressional District, rated Solid Republican by nonpartisan forecasters with a Cook PVI of R+5, anchors trader consensus at 89% for a GOP victory in the House election winner market. Lidonnici won a competitive Democratic primary but faces steep historical odds in a district Buchanan carried by 23 points in 2022. Absent recent polls or scandals shifting dynamics, traders price minimal upset risk ahead of the November 5 ballot, though voter turnout remains a wildcard factor.

question: Will the Republican Party win the FL-15 House seat?
description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the FL-15 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Anna Paulina Luna's dominant position drives the 81% trader consensus for a GOP hold in Florida's 16th Congressional District House race, bolstered by the district's R+3 partisan lean and her strong 2022 performance. Odds reflect stable trader assessments ahead of the November 5 general election.

eventMetadata.context_description: In Florida's 17th Congressional District, trader consensus strongly favors the Republican incumbent Greg Steube at 86.5%, driven by the district's solid GOP lean (Cook Partisan Voter Index R+9) and consistent polling leads of 20+ points over Democrat Andrea Colina. Recent developments include Steube's full recovery from a January ladder fall, active campaigning, and statewide GOP momentum from Trump's 2024 win, widening the gap; no major shifts noted, with election day votes pending certification.
